title: Cell structure — what's inside a cell?
examTip: AQA tests cell structure on almost every Paper 1. You need the organelles of animal cells, plant cells AND bacterial cells — with the function of each. The eukaryote vs prokaryote distinction is a guaranteed question.
introTips: This lesson covers the organelles inside animal, plant and bacterial cells — and what each one actually does.
You'll also learn the key difference between eukaryotic cells (animal and plant) and prokaryotic cells (bacteria) — this is a frequent exam target.
By the end you should be able to draw and label a basic animal cell, a plant cell, and a bacterial cell from memory.
intro: Every living thing — from a bacterium to a blue whale — is made of cells. But not all cells are the same. Some have a nucleus locked inside a membrane. Some have chloroplasts. Some are just a bag of DNA with no membrane around it at all. This lesson maps out what's inside a cell, why each part is there, and how different types of cell compare.

keyPoints.heading: The organelles in an animal cell
keyPoints.content: Animal cells are eukaryotic — they have a proper nucleus surrounded by a membrane. Inside the nucleus is the DNA, which contains the instructions for making proteins and controlling everything the cell does. The cytoplasm is the gel-like liquid where most chemical reactions happen. The cell membrane controls what enters and leaves the cell. Mitochondria are where aerobic respiration happens — they release energy from glucose. Ribosomes are where proteins are made, using the instructions from DNA.
writeBullets: Nucleus — contains DNA; controls cell activities
Cytoplasm — site of most chemical reactions
Cell membrane — controls what enters and leaves
Mitochondria — site of aerobic respiration; releases energy

keyTerms.def: A cell with a membrane-bound nucleus — includes all animal and plant cells
Organelles where aerobic respiration occurs, releasing energy for the cell
Tiny organelles where proteins are made (synthesised) using instructions from DNA

keyPoints.memTip: Five animal cell organelles: Nucleus (control), Cytoplasm (reactions), Cell membrane (boundary), Mitochondria (energy), Ribosomes (proteins). Use the sentence: 'No Cat Can Make Rabbits' — Nucleus, Cytoplasm, Cell membrane, Mitochondria, Ribosomes. Cover your notes and write all five with functions.

keyPoints.heading: What plant cells have that animal cells don't
keyPoints.content: Plant cells have all the animal cell organelles, plus three extras. The cell wall is made of cellulose and sits outside the cell membrane — it's rigid, supports the cell, and stops it bursting when it takes in water. Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, the green pigment that absorbs light energy for photosynthesis. The permanent vacuole is a large, fluid-filled space in the centre — it pushes the cytoplasm outwards, keeping the cell firm (turgid). Not all plant cells have chloroplasts — only the green parts that get light.
writeBullets: Cell wall (cellulose) — rigid; gives support; prevents bursting
Chloroplasts — contain chlorophyll; site of photosynthesis
Permanent vacuole — filled with cell sap; maintains turgor pressure
Root cells have NO chloroplasts (no light underground)

keyTerms.def: Rigid outer layer of plant cells made of cellulose; gives support and shape
Organelle in plant cells containing chlorophyll; absorbs light for photosynthesis
Large fluid-filled space in plant cells; maintains the cell's firm shape

keyPoints.memTip: Three plant extras: Wall (cellulose), Chloroplasts (photosynthesis), Vacuole (turgidity). If asked 'what do plant cells have that animal cells don't?' — those three. Never say all plant cells have chloroplasts — root cells don't. That one costs marks every year.

keyPoints.heading: Bacterial cells — the prokaryote
keyPoints.content: Bacteria are prokaryotic — their genetic material is NOT enclosed in a nucleus. Instead, they have a single circular loop of DNA floating free in the cytoplasm. They may also have small, separate rings of DNA called plasmids. Like all cells, they have a cell membrane and ribosomes (though bacterial ribosomes are smaller than those in eukaryotic cells). They have a cell wall — but it is NOT made of cellulose (it's a different substance). Bacteria have no mitochondria and no chloroplasts. They are much smaller than eukaryotic cells — typically 1–10 micrometres.
writeBullets: Genetic material: circular loop of DNA in the cytoplasm
May also have plasmids (small, extra rings of DNA)
Has: cell membrane, ribosomes, cell wall (NOT cellulose)
Much smaller than eukaryotic cells (~1–10 µm)

keyTerms.def: A cell without a membrane-bound nucleus — bacteria are the main example
Small, circular loop of DNA found in bacteria, separate from the main chromosomal DNA
The single loop of genetic material in a bacterial cell, not enclosed in a nucleus

keyPoints.memTip: Prokaryote = no proper nucleus. Bacterial cell has: circular DNA + (maybe) plasmids + cell membrane + ribosomes + cell wall. It does NOT have: nucleus, mitochondria, chloroplasts. If an exam asks 'how does a bacterial cell differ from a plant cell?' — three things are missing (nucleus, mitochondria, chloroplasts) AND the cell wall is a different material.

keyPoints.heading: Eukaryotic vs prokaryotic — the key comparison
keyPoints.content: The fundamental split in biology is between cells that have a membrane-bound nucleus (eukaryotes) and cells that don't (prokaryotes). All animal cells, plant cells, and fungi are eukaryotic. All bacteria are prokaryotic. Eukaryotic cells are generally larger, and appeared on Earth around 1.5 billion years after prokaryotes. Prokaryotes were the first life forms. The word 'eukaryote' means 'true kernel' — that kernel is the nucleus.
writeBullets: Eukaryotic: membrane-bound nucleus — animals, plants, fungi
Prokaryotes appeared on Earth first (oldest life forms)
Eukaryotes are generally larger than prokaryotes

keyTerms.def: Organism whose cells have a membrane-bound nucleus — animals, plants, fungi
Organism whose cells lack a membrane-bound nucleus — bacteria

keyPoints.memTip: Pro = before, karyon = nucleus. Prokaryote = before nucleus (no proper nucleus). Eukaryote = true nucleus (has one). Animals, plants, fungi = eukaryotes. Bacteria = prokaryotes. That sentence is the most commonly tested fact in this topic.

tables.title: Comparing animal, plant and bacterial cells

1.question: Which organelle is the site of aerobic respiration in an animal cell?
1.correctFeedback: Correct — mitochondria release energy from glucose through aerobic respiration.
1.wrongFeedback: The mitochondria are where aerobic respiration happens and energy is released. The nucleus controls the cell. Ribosomes make proteins. The cell membrane controls entry and exit.

3.question: What is the key difference between eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells?
options: Prokaryotes do not have a membrane-bound nucleus
3.correctFeedback: Exactly — prokaryotes have no membrane-bound nucleus. Their DNA floats free as a circular loop in the cytoplasm.
3.wrongFeedback: The defining difference is the nucleus. Prokaryotes (like bacteria) have no membrane-bound nucleus — their DNA is a circular loop in the cytoplasm. Eukaryotes (animals, plants) do have a proper nucleus. Prokaryotes are actually smaller, not larger.

commonMistakes: Saying bacteria have a nucleus — they don't. They have circular DNA floating in the cytoplasm, with no membrane around it.
Saying all plant cells have chloroplasts — root cells have none because they're underground and never exposed to light.

Confusing the cell wall and cell membrane — they're different structures. The membrane controls what enters and leaves. The wall gives rigid support. Plant cells have both. Animal cells only have a membrane.
Saying the bacterial cell wall is made of cellulose — it isn't. Only plant cell walls are cellulose. The bacterial cell wall is a different material entirely.
Forgetting ribosomes in bacterial cells — bacteria DO have ribosomes (they need to make proteins), but they're smaller than eukaryotic ones.
examTips: For any 'compare' question, build a table in your head: feature by feature, cell by cell. Tick or cross each one. AQA loves the table format for this topic.
The most-tested single fact: prokaryotes do not have a membrane-bound nucleus. If you remember nothing else, remember that.
When asked to describe a plant cell, list all FIVE features (including the three extras). Listing only animal cell organelles loses marks every time.
revisionCardBullets: Nucleus — contains DNA; controls cell (membrane-bound in eukaryotes only)
Mitochondria — site of aerobic respiration; releases energy from glucose
Ribosomes — site of protein synthesis; present in ALL cells
Cell membrane — controls what enters and leaves; present in ALL cells
Cell wall (cellulose) — plant cells only; rigid support
Chloroplasts — plant cells only (green parts); site of photosynthesis
Permanent vacuole — plant cells only; maintains turgidity
Prokaryote = no membrane-bound nucleus; circular DNA + possible plasmids
Bacteria = prokaryotes: have cell wall (NOT cellulose), ribosomes, no mitochondria
